Common-Ion Effect

Beer-Lambert Law
Faraday's laws relate electrons transferred, electrode mass, current, time, and ionic charge.
Mass measurements connect to particle counts in chemical reactions.
Mass spectra identify element isotopes and their relative abundances.
Pure substances contain molecules or ions in fixed proportions.
Pure substances have one type of particle; mixtures contain multiple types in variable proportions.
Atoms contain electrons, protons, and neutrons in nucleus and electron regions.
PES peak position indicates ionization energy; peak height indicates electron count.
Periodic table organization reflects electron configuration patterns and shell filling.
Chemical bond formation depends on valence electron and nuclear interactions.
Electronegativity increases right and up in periodic table trends.
Potential energy graphs show equilibrium bond length and bond energy relationships.
Ionic crystals arrange cations and anions systematically to optimize electrostatic forces.
Metallic bonding features positive ions surrounded by mobile valence electrons.
Lewis diagrams follow systematic rules for construction and representation.
Resonance structures refine Lewis diagrams for accurate molecular predictions.
VSEPR predicts electron pair arrangement via Coulombic repulsion.
London dispersion forces result from temporary dipole Coulombic interactions between molecules.
Intermolecular force strength determines vapor pressure, boiling point, and melting point.
Solids are crystalline or amorphous with limited particle motion.
Ideal gas law relates pressure, volume, moles, and temperature.
KMT connects observable gas properties to particle motion and energy distribution.
Real gases deviate from ideal behavior due to intermolecular forces and molecular volume effects.
Solutions are homogeneous mixtures with uniform properties; heterogeneous mixtures have varying properties.
Particulate models show solution structure through component concentrations and interactions.
Solutions require chromatography methods exploiting differential intermolecular interactions for separation.
Similar intermolecular forces promote solubility and miscibility between substances.
Spectral regions correspond to specific molecular or electronic transitions.
Photon absorption or emission changes atomic/molecular energy by photon energy.
Beer-Lambert law equation A = εbc relates light absorption to molar absorptivity, path length, concentration.
Physical changes alter properties without changing composition or substance identity.
Balanced equations represent all physical and chemical processes symbolically.
Translate balanced equations into symbolic particulate molecular representations.
Chemical processes break/form bonds; physical processes alter intermolecular interactions only.
Atom conservation allows calculation of products from reactants or vice versa.
Titrations determine analyte amount; equivalence point occurs at complete consumption.
Acid-base reactions transfer protons between chemical species.
Brønsted-Lowry acids donate protons; bases accept protons.
Construct balanced redox equations by combining oxidation and reduction half-reactions.
Chemical kinetics measures how quickly reactants convert to products over time.
Experimental methods monitor reactant/product amounts to determine reaction rates.
Reaction order determined from concentration-time graphs.
Elementary reaction rate laws derive directly from collision stoichiometry.
Successful collisions between reactants initiate bond-breaking and bond-making.
Elementary reactions break existing bonds and form new bonds.
Reaction mechanisms are sequential elementary steps with reactants, intermediates, products, catalysts.
Rate law is determined by the rate-limiting step's molecularity in mechanisms.
Use pre-equilibrium approximation when first step is not rate-limiting.
Elementary reaction energetics enable construction of multistep reaction energy profiles.
Catalysts increase reaction rates by raising collision frequency or lowering activation energy.
Temperature shifts reveal whether a system gains or loses energy.
Energy diagrams represent processes as endothermic or exothermic.
Warmer bodies have particles with greater average kinetic energy.
Heat transfer between bodies quantified by q=mcΔT; calorimetry measures heat transfer.
Energy transfer during phase changes causes energy increases or decreases; temperature remains constant.
Enthalpy change indicates heat released or absorbed during reaction.
Chemical reactions involve breaking and forming bonds, altering system potential energy.
Use standard enthalpy of formation tables to calculate reaction enthalpy changes.
Complex processes decompose into individual steps, each with distinct energy changes.
Reversible processes occur in physical and chemical systems including phase changes and reactions.
Net reaction direction depends on relative forward and reverse reaction rates.
Qc and Qp describe relative concentrations or pressures at any time; at equilibrium, Q equals K.
Kc and Kp are calculated from experimental equilibrium concentration or pressure data.
Large K values drive reactions to completion; small K values inhibit reaction progression.
Reversing a reaction inverts its equilibrium constant value.
Equilibrium concentrations predictable from initial conditions and equilibrium constant.
Particulate models show particle ratios before and at equilibrium, revealing K value.
Le Châtelier's principle predicts equilibrium system responses to various stresses.
Disturbances make Q ≠ K; system responds to restore equilibrium.
Salt dissolution is reversible; Ksp describes its extent.
Common ion presence reduces salt solubility; explain via Le Châtelier or Ksp calculations.
pH and pOH quantify hydronium and hydroxide ion concentrations logarithmically.
Strong acids completely ionize; [H3O+] equals initial acid concentration.
Weak acids partially ionize; most molecules remain un-ionized in solution.
Strong acid-strong base mixtures react quantitatively; pH depends on excess reagent.
Titration curves plot pH versus titrant volume to summarize controlled acid-base reactions.
Molecular structure determines acid-base strength through conjugate base stabilization effects.
Comparing solution pH to pKa predicts whether acid or base form predominates.
Buffers contain conjugate acid-base pairs; conjugate acid neutralizes added base, conjugate base neutralizes added acid.
Buffer pH depends on pKa and conjugate acid-base pair concentration ratio.
Higher buffer component concentrations increase capacity while maintaining constant pH.
Salt solubility depends on pH when ions are weak acids, bases, or hydroxide.
Entropy increases when matter disperses; particles move freely occupying larger volumes.
Calculate entropy change using absolute entropies of products minus reactants.
Standard Gibbs free energy (ΔG°) measures reactions with all reactants/products in standard states.
Thermodynamically favorable reactions may proceed at immeasurably slow rates.
Negative ΔG° indicates products favored at equilibrium with K > 1.
ΔG° dissolution depends on lattice energy, solvation reorganization, solute-solvent interactions; predictions challenging due to cancellations.
External energy sources drive thermodynamically unfavorable processes like electrolysis and photosynthesis.
Cell components have specific roles; operational characteristics visible macroscopically and particulate level.
Electrochemical cells contain redox reactions that are either thermodynamically favored or unfavored.
Cell potential depends on concentrations and distance from equilibrium.
At equilibrium, concentrations and partial pressures remain constant with no observable changes.
Particulate models show how intermolecular interactions create macroscopic properties.
Weak acid-strong base mixtures form buffers, excess base, or salt solutions with predictable pH.
Kinetic control occurs when high activation energy prevents thermodynamically favorable reactions from proceeding measurably.
K and ΔG° relate through exponential and logarithmic mathematical equations.
Catalysts are consumed and regenerated, maintaining constant net concentration throughout.
Stoichiometry determines the relative rates of reactant and product concentration changes.
Salt dissolution breaks ionic bonds while forming ion-dipole interactions; classification ambiguous.
Equivalence point moles of titrant equal moles of analyte; determines analyte concentration.
Energy changes manifest as endothermic or exothermic processes in heating, cooling, phase changes, and reactions.
Weak acid equilibrium described by Ka or pKa; pH determined from concentration and pKa.
Particle collisions transfer energy between bodies in thermal contact.
Energy is conserved in all chemical and physical processes.
Rate law expresses reaction rate proportional to reactant concentrations raised to powers.
Chemical equations show atom rearrangement; mass and charge conservation demonstrated.
Chemical changes create new substances; heat, light, gas, precipitate, color indicate change.
Energy absorbed in forward phase changes equals energy released in reverse phase changes.
Exothermic reactions release heat; endothermic reactions absorb heat until equilibrium.
Acid-base indicators change properties like color based on protonation state and pH.
Average atomic mass calculated from weighted isotopic masses and abundances.
Sum bond energies to calculate enthalpy change; compare energy released versus required.
Mass ratios of elements in a compound remain constant.
Elemental analysis reveals atomic ratios in substances and assesses sample purity.
Avogadro's number converts moles to actual particle counts.
Coulomb's law quantifies electrostatic force between charged particles inversely with distance squared.
Redox reactions transfer electrons; combustion with O₂ produces CO₂ and H₂O from hydrocarbons.
Net enthalpy change equals sum of individual enthalpy changes due to energy conservation.
Element position predicts periodicity trends via effective nuclear charge concepts.
Le Châtelier's principle does not apply to electrochemical systems.
Same group elements form similar compounds with analogous properties.
Multiplying stoichiometric coefficients by factor c raises K to power c.
Similar electronegativity atoms form nonpolar covalent bonds.
With constant path length and wavelength, absorbance is proportional only to concentration.
Higher bond order and smaller atomic core produce shorter, stronger bonds.
Coupling unfavorable reactions to favorable ones via shared intermediates yields favorable overall ΔG°.
Buffer composition ratio determines whether it neutralizes added acid or base better.
Interstitial alloys have smaller atoms filling spaces between larger atoms.
Three-or-more particle simultaneous collisions in elementary reactions are uncommon.
Solids and pure liquids are excluded from reaction quotient expressions.
Octet rule and formal charge select optimal Lewis structures.
Galvanic cells are thermodynamically favored; electrolytic cells are unfavored; diagrams identify reactions.
Lewis diagrams and VSEPR predict geometry, angles, bond energies, dipoles, hybridization.
First-order reactions show linear ln[concentration] versus time plots.
Entropy increases when energy disperses; higher temperature broadens kinetic energy distribution.
Polar molecule dipole moments create dipole-dipole and ion-dipole interactions stronger than dispersion forces.
Calculate solubility from Ksp; compare relative solubilities of substances.
Standard cell potential is calculated from half-reaction reduction potentials.
Wavelength, frequency, and light speed relate via c = λν; photon energy via E = hν.
Concentration changes Q; temperature changes K; redistribution restores equality.
Liquid particles are in close contact, continually moving and colliding.
Partial pressures are independent; total pressure equals sum of partial pressures.
Particles move randomly; kinetic energy depends on mass and velocity.
Water in aqueous solutions accepts and donates protons in acid-base reactions.
Q versus K determines reaction direction; Q = K indicates dynamic equilibrium.
Equation coefficients show proportional relationships for molar calculations.
Molarity is the standard laboratory expression for solution composition.
ΔG° < 0 indicates thermodynamically favored processes; avoid misleading 'spontaneous' term.
Le Châtelier's principle predicts stress effects on measurable solution properties.
Successful collisions require sufficient energy and proper orientation for rearrangement.
Water autoionization is characterized by Kw equaling 1.0 × 10−14 at 25°C.
Reaction coordinate plots the complex motions rearranging reactants into products.
Combined elementary steps must equal the overall balanced chemical equation.
Strong bases completely dissociate; [OH−] depends on group I or II origin.
Ionic solids are brittle with high melting points; conduct electricity when molten.
Cell potential magnitude decreases as system approaches equilibrium; direction determined by Q relative to K.
Thermal equilibrium occurs when both bodies have equal average kinetic energy.
Molecular, complete ionic, and net ionic equations serve different contextual purposes.
Calculate ΔG° from standard free energies of formation of products and reactants.
Adding reactions multiplies their individual equilibrium constants together.
Coulomb's law: larger charges and smaller distances strengthen ionic interactions.
Solids and liquids have similar molar volumes due to close particle contact.
Neutral solutions have pH = pOH = 7.0 at 25°C with pKw = 14.
Stoichiometry integrates with gas laws and molarity for quantitative analysis.
Different substances show different temperature changes from same thermal energy input.
Oxidation always occurs at anode; reduction always occurs at cathode.
Reversing reactions negates ΔH; scaling multiplies ΔH; summing reactions adds ΔH values.
Temperature directly corresponds to average particle kinetic energy.
Dipole-dipole and ion-dipole force strength depend on partial charge signs and orientation.
Periodic trends enable property value prediction without experimental data.
Solubility rules quantitatively relate to Ksp; Ksp>1 means soluble.
Reactant powers are orders; sum of powers equals overall reaction order.
ΔG° near zero gives K ≈ 1; extreme ΔG° values cause K to deviate strongly.
Lewis structures have limitations, especially with odd electron counts.
Electrons transfer from oxidized species to reduced species in redox reactions.
Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution estimates fraction of sufficiently energetic collisions versus temperature.
Second-order reactions show linear 1/[concentration] versus time plots.
Bond breaking/forming changes potential energy, causing observable temperature changes.
Energy profile shows reactants, transition state, products; activation energy is reactant-to-transition state difference.
Ionization in water allows identification and strength comparison of conjugate pairs.
Standard free energy change relates negatively to cell potential via ΔGo = −nFEo.
Atomic mass units numerically equal molar mass in grams.
Intermediates are produced then consumed, existing only during reaction.
Ionic compound charges are determined by valence electrons and periodic table position.
Graphs effectively illustrate relationships among pressure, volume, temperature, and moles.
Weak base-strong acid mixtures form buffers, excess acid, or salt solutions with predictable pH.
Substitutional alloys have similar-sized atoms replacing each other in lattice.
Unequal electronegativity creates polar bonds on ionic-covalent continuum.
Empirical formula shows lowest whole number atom ratios.
Catalysts binding to reactants improves orientation or lowers activation energy via intermediates.
Select indicators with pKa near the equivalence point pH for accurate titrations.
Weak bases partially ionize; most molecules remain un-ionized in solution.
Hybridization describes electron arrangement; sp/sp2/sp3 have 180°/120°/109.5° angles.
Equilibrium is dynamic with forward and reverse reactions occurring at equal rates.
Exothermic reactions release energy to surroundings; endothermic reactions absorb energy from surroundings.
Reaction rate depends on concentration, temperature, surface area, catalysts, and environment.
At half-equivalence point, conjugate pair concentrations equal; pH equals pKa.
Electrons occupy shells and subshells; core and valence electrons explained by Aufbau principle.
Both ΔH° and ΔS° must be considered for some processes like water freezing.
Rate constant is temperature-dependent with units reflecting overall reaction order.
Slopes from concentration plots determine rate constants for orders zero, one, two.
Weak base equilibrium described by Kb or pKb; pH determined from concentration and pKb.
Reaction rate increases with temperature; more collisions reach transition state energy.
Detecting intermediates experimentally supports specific reaction mechanisms.
Weak acid-weak base mixtures reach equilibrium; neither reagent fully consumed.
Covalent bonding catalysts form new intermediates via proton transfer or bond formation.
Solution formation is exothermic or endothermic based on intermolecular interaction strength changes.
Strong acid/base titrations yield neutral pH at equivalence; weak acid/base titrations yield basic/acidic.
Heating increases system energy; cooling decreases system energy.
Graphical analysis of concentration or pressure versus time reveals equilibrium establishment.
Negative ΔG° favors products; positive ΔG° favors reactants.
Ionization energy depends on electron distance from nucleus and effective nuclear charge.
Metal-nonmetal bonds ionic; nonmetal-nonmetal covalent; properties confirm type.
Orbital overlap forms sigma and pi bonds; sigma stronger; pi prevents rotation.
Hydrogen bonding occurs between H bonded to N, O, F and electronegative atoms.
Network solids have high melting points; three-dimensional structures are hard and rigid.
Gas particles move constantly with no definite volume or shape.
K and Q share identical mathematical forms; algebraic rules apply to both.
Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution graphs particle energy and velocity distributions.
Use Nernst equation qualitatively; rote calculation alone insufficient for understanding.
Oxidation numbers identify oxidized and reduced species in redox reactions.
Use molar solubility of saturated solutions to calculate Ksp.
Kw varies with temperature, so neutral pH deviates from 7.0 above or below 25°C.
Molecular solids have low melting points and don't conduct electricity.
Large biomolecules exhibit noncovalent interactions within and between molecules.
Metallic solids have delocalized valence electrons across atoms.
Both specific and molar heat capacities are used in energy calculations.
Polyprotic acid titration curves reveal number of protons and each associated pKa.
Calculate ΔG° using enthalpy, entropy, and absolute temperature relationship.
Surface catalysts form bound intermediates through adsorption or covalent surface bonding.
Percent ionization calculated from pKa/pKb and initial concentration or equilibrium concentrations.
First-order reactions have constant half-life related to rate constant.
Precipitation forms insoluble compounds; Na, K, NH₄, and NO₃ salts are soluble.
Comparing initial rates determines reaction order for each reactant.
Conjugate acid-base pair constants related through Kw relationship.
Systems change energy via heating/cooling, phase transitions, and chemical reactions.
Metallic solids conduct electricity and are malleable; interstitial alloys reduce ductility.
Radioactive decay exemplifies first-order reaction kinetics.
Predict thermodynamic favorability at different temperatures from signs of ΔH° and ΔS°.
Temperature change in dissolution calorimetry indicates exothermic or endothermic processes.
Noncovalent interactions determine the shape and properties of biomolecules.
